summary: hpsetup CLI fetches unverified tarballs from anonymous.xyz CDN and persists the channel into CI
details: The `hpsetup` CLI downloads tarballs from `https://hpsetup-cdn.932324.xyz/api/tarball/<slug>/<version>?key=<userKey>` (src/constants.js:16, src/download.js:24) and extracts them with strip:1 over `node_modules/@heroui-pro/react` and `node_modules/heroui-native-pro`, overwriting their `dist`/`lib` directories. Those bytes are then loaded by the consumer's application at build/run time. There is no integrity verification, no version pinning beyond the server's response, and the CDN host is a numeric `.xyz` domain unrelated to the heroui.com brand the package advertises (LOGO 'HP SETUP', references to @heroui-pro/react). The unscoped package name `hpsetup` plus mismatched infrastructure leaves the publisher unverifiable. Additionally, src/vercel.js:18-29 writes `installCommand: 'npx -y hpsetup@latest <userKey>'` into the consumer's `vercel.json`, persisting the remote-fetch channel so every subsequent Vercel deploy re-pulls fresh tarballs from the same anonymous CDN, and embeds the user's key in the repo. When `process.env.CI` is set, src/install.js:75-83 + src/trust.js:91-100 enumerates every package in `node_modules` with install/postinstall/preinstall/prebuild scripts and unconditionally adds all of them to `pnpm.onlyBuiltDependencies` / `trustedDependencies`, globally disabling pnpm/bun's postinstall gating for arbitrary unrelated packages and materially weakening the consumer's supply-chain defenses. The combination — opaque publisher + unpinned mutable code delivery into node_modules + persistence into CI + blanket trust-list weakening — is a long-lived install-time RCE channel into the consumer's build pipeline.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
